title: The Job of a College President
significance: The founding document of learning engineering. Simon coined the term 'learning engineer' and argued that teaching effectiveness is a domain of expertise distinct from subject-matter knowledge, requiring its own scientific foundation in cognitive science. The philosophical and historical origin of everything in the field.

title: Online Education: A Catalyst for Higher Education Reforms
significance: Re-introduced 'learning engineer' to higher education discourse at the moment MOOCs reached 58M+ global students. Recommended universities create dedicated learning engineering roles. Widely cited as the document that triggered the modern LE movement in higher education institutions.

title: High-Leverage Opportunities for Learning Engineering
publisher: University of Pennsylvania Center for Learning Analytics
significance: Synthesized a 2020 convening of 100+ academics, policymakers, and practitioners to define ten strategic priorities in three domains: Better LE Infrastructure, Supporting Human Processes, and Better Learning Technologies. The field's most comprehensive contemporary roadmap and primary policy document framing LE's current agenda.

title: Artificial Intelligence and the Future of Teaching and Learning: Insights and Recommendations
authors: U.S. Department of Education, Office of Educational Technology
significance: First major federal policy document addressing AI in education with explicit civil-rights framing. Documented algorithmic bias in e-proctoring systems and mandated algorithmic fairness and data representation requirements. Sets the ethical and legal floor for LE practice in the US.

title: EDUCAUSE Horizon Report: Teaching and Learning Edition
significance: The annual predictive literature for edtech adoption across global higher education. Tracks short-, mid-, and long-term adoption horizons. Documents the gradual mainstreaming of LE concepts (analytics, adaptive systems, AI tutors) from forecast to widespread adoption across the 2017–2024 arc.

title: NMC Horizon Report: 2017 Higher Education Edition
significance: Identified adaptive learning technologies and data-driven personalization as short-term adoption trends in higher education. Forecast the long-term restructuring of degrees around competency measurement. Part of the annual Horizon series that documented LE's gradual integration into mainstream institutional practice.

title: Learning Engineering Toolkit: Evidence-Based Practices from the Learning Sciences, Instructional Design, and Beyond
significance: The field's primary practitioner handbook. Frames LE as a verb — an iterative, evidence-based problem-solving process — not a technology set or a job title. Covers lean-agile methods, HCI design, data instrumentation, motivation modeling, and predictive analytics. Technology-agnostic: principles apply equally to AI-driven software and low-tech interventions.

title: Learning Engineering for Online Education: Theoretical Contexts and Design-Based Examples
significance: First book-length treatment of learning engineering applied to digital and distributed contexts. Saxberg's formulation — LE as applying learning science at 'massive, affordable, data-rich scale' — became widely adopted. Documents how online architectures enable continuous telemetry that peer-reviewed journals document years later.

title: Learning Engineering Adoption Maturity Model (LEAMM)
significance: Organizational capability matrix allowing enterprises to assess and advance their LE practice across human-centered design, learning science integration, and data-driven iteration dimensions. Provides a structured pathway from ad-hoc instructional design toward mature learning engineering practice. The field's primary organizational self-assessment tool.

title: OpenSimon Toolkit
authors: Carnegie Mellon University, The Simon Initiative
significance: Flagship open infrastructure for LE research globally. Comprises DataShop, LearnSphere, CTAT, OLI, DiscourseDB, LightSide, Bazaar, Mathtutor, ChemCollective, and Echo. Enables researchers worldwide to build, test, and study LE systems without proprietary infrastructure.

title: 7 Things You Should Know About Learning Engineering
significance: Widely circulated practitioner-oriented brief in the canonical EDUCAUSE '7 Things' series, introduced LE to a broad institutional technology audience including CIOs, provosts, and instructional technology directors. Accelerated LE vocabulary adoption in non-research higher education contexts.

title: Learning Engineering (IEEE Press)
significance: The canonical textbook of the learning engineering field. Two-part structure covering LE foundations and practice. End notes are a primary seed source for corpus expansion — over 200 references spanning cognitive science, instructional design, analytics, and systems engineering. IEEE Press, 2022. Required reading for any LE practitioner or researcher.
notes: PRIMARY seed document. Harvest all chapter end notes for corpus expansion.

title: IEEE ICICLE Resources Page
significance: Curated resource list maintained by the IEEE ICICLE community. Includes papers, tools, programs, and community resources. The most authoritative practitioner-curated collection of LE resources, updated continuously as the field evolves.

title: How People Learn: Brain, Mind, Experience, and School (Expanded Edition)
authors: National Academies of Sciences, Engineering, and Medicine
significance: The National Academies synthesis of cognitive science, neuroscience, and educational research that established how people learn. Free online. Widely cited as the most authoritative accessible synthesis of learning science. Essential T01 foundation layer and often the starting point for practitioners new to learning engineering.

title: The Forgotten Role of the Learning Engineer
significance: Canonical entry-point essay calling for the learning engineering profession. Argued that education is the 'last great unapplied science' and that learning engineers — practitioners who apply learning science rigorously to educational products — are urgently needed at scale. Widely cited; often assigned as introductory reading for LE courses and programs.

title: ICICLE Body of Knowledge (BoK) — current draft
significance: The field's emerging canonical definition document. Defines competency areas, vocabulary, and practice standards for learning engineering. Provides the official LEBOK framework that this corpus is organized to support. Version tracking required — update when new versions released.

title: MIL-STD-1472H: Human Engineering Design Criteria for Military Systems
significance: DoD standard specifying human engineering design criteria for military systems, equipment, and facilities. The authoritative source for human factors requirements in defense training systems. Directly applicable to LE practitioners designing or procuring training systems in military and government contexts.

title: NASA Human Systems Integration Practitioner's Guide
significance: NASA's operational guide to implementing HSI across the systems engineering lifecycle. Demonstrates how human-centered analysis (task analysis, workload, error analysis) integrates with traditional SE milestones. Applicable to complex LE system development in high-consequence environments.

title: INCOSE Systems Engineering Handbook: A Guide for System Life Cycle Processes and Activities (4th ed.)
significance: The authoritative practitioner reference for systems engineering processes and lifecycle management. Provides the SE process framework (requirements, architecture, integration, verification, validation) that learning engineering adapts for learning system development. Essential for LE practitioners with systems engineering backgrounds.

title: DoD Instruction 1322.26: Development, Management, and Delivery of Distributed Learning
significance: DoD policy governing the design, management, and delivery of distributed (technology-based) learning for military training. Mandates ADDIE-based instructional systems design, learning objective taxonomies, and evaluation standards. The governance framework for LE deployment across the largest single training system in the world.

title: State of the Industry Report
significance: Annual survey of corporate training investment, delivery modalities, and effectiveness measurement across hundreds of organizations. Provides LE practitioners with benchmarks for training hours, costs, technology adoption, and evaluation maturity. The most widely cited industry data on the state of organizational learning and development.

title: AC 120-51E: Crew Resource Management Training
significance: FAA advisory circular defining standards for Crew Resource Management (CRM) training programs. CRM training is one of the most rigorous evidence-based training programs ever deployed at scale — a landmark case study in applying learning science to high-stakes professional training. Directly relevant to LE practitioners in aviation, healthcare simulation, and safety-critical training domains.

title: What Works Clearinghouse Handbook (v5.0)
authors: Institute of Education Sciences (IES), U.S. Department of Education
significance: The authoritative evidence standards framework for evaluating education research. Specifies the criteria an intervention study must meet to be considered high-quality evidence (randomized design, statistical power, attrition standards). Sets the evidentiary bar that LE evaluation must meet to influence policy and procurement decisions.

title: Human Systems Integration in the System Development Process: A New Look
significance: National Academies report synthesizing research on integrating human factors into the system development process. Provides the HSI framework (human factors engineering, manpower, personnel, training, safety) that LE borrows when treating learning as a human-system performance problem. Foundational for LE practitioners working in defense and complex system contexts.

title: Siemens Pathway to Learning Engineering
significance: Cloud-based, project-based STEM curriculum and educator professional development introducing engineering design and industry tools for secondary classrooms. Industry-sponsored LE pathway demonstrating how corporate partnerships can fund learning engineering curriculum at scale. Complements university LE pathways.
